narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like choosing full-time study and coming from a middle-income family. The admissions model we use predicted a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—it's designed to identify students likely to pass and succeed in the program. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your lower decile ranking in Year 3 grades compared to most students, who typically rank much higher around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile was also on the lower side relative to averages, adding to the model's concern about your trajectory toward bar success. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as stronger than many applicants', helping to push the prediction toward passing rather than failing. Your LSAT score, while below the typical high averages seen in the dataset, provided a small counterbalance in the same favorable direction. Compared to typical students—who often have top-half deciles, higher LSATs around 37, and GPAs just above 3.2—your profile raised flags on early and later school performance, tipping the scales toward higher failure risk. Family income in the middle bracket played a minor role in increasing that risk, unlike some higher-income peers. While full-time enrollment is the norm and helped slightly, it wasn't enough to offset the key academic concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers—many factors beyond these metrics matter deeply. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that addresses these areas. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the ranks of successful lawyers. Keep pushing forward.
